text.title: Alert Noise Budget — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn alerting from "more is safer" into "exceeding budget means tuning, not adding".

rule.statement: Every service MUST have a documented noise budget with two numbers: max page-rate per on-call shift (default: 2) and max non-actionable alerts per 7-day rolling window (default: 5). The budget is per-service so noisy upstream services do not blow downstream services' budgets.

rationale.source: Google SRE Book ch. 6, Honeycomb alert-fatigue research

rule.rationale: Aggregate budgets hide which service is the source. Per-service budgets create accountability and let the tuning conversation be specific. Defaults (2 / 5) come from Google's on-call data — beyond this, responder accuracy and morale collapse measurably.

rule.statement: Every alert acknowledgment MUST include an actionable yes/no flag with a one-line reason. "Yes" = responder took a remediation action (rollback, scale, patch, manual ops). "No" = noise (transient, self-healing, false positive, duplicate). Default to "no" if not explicitly marked yes.

rationale.source: Operational research on attribution, PagerDuty annual on-call reports

rule.rationale: Without the flag, "noise" is impressionistic. With the flag, the budget metric is computable and decisions are evidence-based. Default-to-no prevents inflation: forces responder to justify "this was real" rather than auto-mark every alert as actionable.

rule.statement: The team reviews budget status weekly. Any service over budget triggers a mandatory tuning action on the top-offending alert: (a) raise threshold, (b) add hysteresis / dampening, (c) add suppression window, OR (d) delete the alert. Adding a NEW alert without retiring or tuning an existing one when over budget is forbidden.

rationale.source: Lean process management, SRE retrospective practice

rule.rationale: Without weekly cadence, noise accumulates. The "must tune before adding" rule prevents the natural drift toward more alerts when stress is high (over-budget signals stress; adding more alerts compounds it).

rule.statement: When in doubt during tuning, the default action is DELETE the alert. The bar to keep an alert is: "I can describe a concrete remediation action a human will take when this fires." If no such action exists, the alert is informational and belongs in a dashboard, not a pager.

rationale.source: Ewaschuk's My Philosophy on Alerting; Charity Majors observability principles

rule.rationale: Most alerts that survive 6 months of "we'll fix it later" exist because deletion feels risky. Empirically, deleting 50% of alerts on a service correlates with FEWER missed incidents because responders trust the survivors. The bar shifts work from page-time to dashboard-time.

rule.statement: A service that exceeds noise budget for 3 consecutive weeks AND has had at least one tuning action per week MUST be escalated to engineering leadership. The escalation is not blame — it's a signal that the service has a deeper reliability problem and needs investment beyond alerting tweaks.

rationale.source: SLO breach escalation patterns, organizational incident-management research

rule.rationale: Chronic noise is rarely a tuning problem alone — it's usually an architectural / dependency / scaling problem. Escalation forces resource allocation before responder burnout. Without escalation, tuning becomes infinite whack-a-mole.

rule.statement: Apply noise budget to one service per pass. Skip this methodology entirely when on-call rotation does not exist or team is unwilling to retire alerts (budget will be permanently exceeded and ignored).
rule.rationale: Anti-conflation: per-service budgets are independent; rolled-up budgets hide per-service patterns.

example.title: Weekly review with budget breach + tuning action
example.code:
Service: billing-api Budget: 2 pages/shift, 5 non-actionable/week This week: Pages: 4 (3 of which non-actionable, marked at ack) Non-actionable total: 9 (over budget by 4)
Top offender: alert "billing-api p95 latency > 800ms" Fired 7 times this week, 6 non-actionable. Root cause of fires: noisy customer cohort hitting batch endpoint; latency recovers within 2 min without intervention.
Tuning action chosen: add hysteresis (must be over threshold for 5 consecutive minutes before paging) + raise threshold to 1200ms. Owner: alex.k Expected impact: 80% noise reduction Follow-up date: 2026-05-27 (next weekly review)
example.note: Illustrates r1-r4: per-service budget, actionable flags drove the analysis, tuning chosen (not deletion this time because the underlying signal is real, just noisy).

example.title: Filter-everything pattern
example.code:
Service: payments-worker Pages this week: 17
Responder action: created a Slack filter that auto-archives all alerts from payments-worker. No alerts deleted. No thresholds tuned. No escalation. "Will look at it later."
Outcome: 2 weeks later, a real payment-stuck incident's alert was also auto-archived. Customer noticed before team did. 6-hour SLA breach.
Repair: enforce r3 and r4. Filter is not a tuning action. Every breach week MUST result in an in-platform change (threshold, hysteresis, suppression, deletion) — not a downstream filter that hides signals.
example.note: Illustrates r3, r4 violations — the classic burnout response that ignores chronic-breach escalation.
